title: Dactylis calycina
taxonomy: Dactylis calycina is a species of grass in the family Poaceae. It is a perennial plant native to Europe and parts of Asia. As a member of the Dactylis genus, Dactylis calycina is closely related to other species of orchard grass. The classification of Dactylis calycina is further divided into the kingdom Plantae and the division Magnoliophyta. This classification reflects the plant's characteristics and evolutionary relationships within the plant kingdom.
uses: Dactylis calycina is a plant species that is not well-documented in modern times. However, its close relative Dactylis glomerata, also known as cock's foot or cock's beard grass, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. This grass is believed to have anti-inflammatory properties and has been used to treat various conditions, including digestive issues and respiratory problems. The plant's leaves and stems have been used to make teas and infusions that are thought to have soothing effects on the body. More research is needed to confirm the medicinal uses of Dactylis calycina specifically.

constituents: Dactylis calycina contains flavonoids, phenolic acids, and sesquiterpene lactones as its active constituents, which contribute to its medicinal and biological properties. These compounds have been reported to have antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities. The flavonoids present in Dactylis calycina, such as kaempferol and quercetin, are responsible for its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. The phenolic acids present in Dactylis calycina, such as caffeic acid and ferulic acid, have been found to have antioxidant and antimicrobial activities.

intro: Dactylis glomerata (also known as Dactylis calycina) is a perennial grass species commonly referred to as orchard grass or cock's foot. The health benefits of Dactylis glomerata include its potential anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may aid in reducing inflammation and oxidative stress in the body.
